turns.content: My CI/CD pipeline keeps failing with a version mismatch error when running cargo build in the workspace. I pasted this message: 'package my-lib v0.1.2 overrides workspace default edition. Expected 2021 but found 2018'. What went wrong?

The error shows that a specific package inside your workspace is overriding the shared configuration defined in Cargo.toml's [workspace] section, which acts as the single source of truth for all members. The issue stems from a misunderstanding: you likely added edition 2018 directly to a member crate's manifest instead of relying on the workspace default. In Rust, when using cargo, the workspace package inheritance means that unless explicitly overridden in an individual [package] section, every member inherits the version, authors, and edition settings from the root. You should remove that manual override so the crate simply inherits the 2021 edition defined at the top level.
Okay, I removed that line and it works now. But why did cargo allow me to set a different edition on just one member? Is that allowed?

Yes, Rust allows explicit overrides in individual package manifests when building larger projects with multiple crates, but using them is generally discouraged for maintenance. The workspace.toml or Cargo.toml [workspace] section serves as the canonical definition that all members should follow to ensure consistency across your entire project. If you let each member define its own edition, you risk drift where one crate accidentally uses 2018 while another uses 2021, breaking tooling and causing errors like the one you just fixed. By inheriting from the workspace, you guarantee that all crates share the same language features without needing to re-specify them.
Hey, I'm new to Rust and working on an open-source project with multiple crates. Why do we need Cargo workspaces? And why does the version in one package affect others?

Good question! A Cargo workspace is like a parent directory that groups multiple packages (like libraries or executables). This lets you share common dependencies, build scripts, and configurations across all members. Now, about your second point — if you set the version in one package under `Cargo.toml`, it propagates to other crates within the same workspace because Cargo treats them as a unified unit during dependency resolution.
So, if I update the version in one crate, do all others get updated automatically? That sounds risky. Why is that not designed differently?

Ah, you're right to be cautious! While Cargo propagates common metadata like version, authors, edition, etc., it doesn't do so recursively for package-specific settings (like dependencies or features). This design choice is intentional because it enforces a single source of truth — all crates inherit the workspace-wide defaults, but each can override as needed. It simplifies maintenance and reduces inconsistency across your project. You should define common metadata in `workspace` section inside the root `Cargo.toml`, while package-specific details stay in their respective `Cargo.toml`s.
